sanskrit:
समं कायशिरोग्रीवं धारयन्नचलं स्थिरः।
संप्रेक्ष्य नासिकाग्रं स्वं दिशश्चानवलोकयन्॥
प्रशान्तात्मा विगतभीर्ब्रह्मचारिव्रते स्थितः।
मनः संयम्य मच्चित्तो युक्त आसीत मत्परः॥
transliteration:
samaṃ kāya-śiro-grīvaṃ dhārayann acalaṃ sthiraḥ |
samprekṣya nāsikāgraṃ svaṃ diśaś cānavalokayan ||
praśāntātmā vigata-bhīr brahmacāri-vrate sthitaḥ |
manaḥ saṃyamya mac-citto yukta āsīta mat-paraḥ ||
literal_meaning: Holding body-head-neck straight and still; gazing at the tip of the nose without looking around; mind tranquil, fear gone, established in the vow of celibacy; subduing the mind, consciousness fixed on Me, the yogī should sit making Me the supreme goal.
contextual_meaning: Krishna prescribes the somatic posture and ethical foundation that stabilize attention for deep meditation: physical alignment calms the nervous system, celibacy channels libido into aspiration, and fearlessness emerges when identity shifts from the ego to the inner witness.
psychological_state: Neuroceptive safety + absorbed concentration; parasympathetic dominance; libidinal energy redirected toward symbolic union with the Self.
trigger_scenario: A client with panic attacks learns to sit spine-long, soften the gaze, and redirect erotic day-dreams into creative work, discovering that bodily stillness and ethical boundaries quiet the amygdala.
anchor_text: Sit upright, gaze softly, steady mind, and aim every breath toward the Divine.
